Document Title: Development Of An Algorithm For Strength Training With Use Haptic Feedback And Artificial Intelligence (AI) Based Enhancement
Authors: E. A. Villegas-Jiménez; A. H. Bello-Guerrero; M. D. Padilla-Torres; L. N. Villagómez-Venegas; C. S. Chávez-Ramírez
Author Affiliations: Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México
Publication Title: 2025 5th International Conference on Electrical, Computer and Energy Technologies (ICECET)
Abstract: The increasing popularity of the use of AI in everyday life has enabled the development of virtual systems for human assistance. This research proposes an implemented linear regression model with a vector of random weights, using a mean square error (MSE), based cost function and regularisation and corresponding Python code that integrates AI-based haptic feedback through vibration, specifically designed to optimise weight training.The results of the simulation model analysis show how key biomechanical parameters are identified and used to improve a person's posture by adjusting technique through real-time vibrational stimuli. The development of the system involves physical benefits such as injury prevention, continuous biomechanical monitoring, adaptive programme structuring and haptic vibration feedback for immediate movement correction. Popular and accurate tools for building linguistic models are mentioned, such as machine learning, computer vision and neural networks. Technological tools such as machine learning, computer vision and neural networks, applied to process biomechanical data and trigger personalised vibrational responses, are highlighted.
Author Keywords: AI;virtual coach;strength training;biomechanics;haptic feedback;python
IEEE Terms: Feedback;Circuits;Oscillators;Rectifiers;Internet of Things;Internet;Communication systems;Protocols;Telecommunication computing;Computer networks

Document Title: Pseudo-Temporal Modeling with 2D CNNs for Framewise Surgical Gesture Segmentation in Robot-Assisted Surgery
Authors: D. Haro-Mendoza; G. A. Castro Vazquez; A. Leonardo Campos Carreras; A. J. Diaz Urias; V. J. Gonzalez-Villela; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; R. E. Arevalo-Ancona
Author Affiliations: Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Biomedical Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 6th International Conference on Communications, Information, Electronic and Energy Systems (CIEES)
Abstract: Surgical procedures can be decomposed into elementary units (tasks, surgemes, and dexemes) that represent the building blocks of surgical performance. Segmenting gestures at this level is crucial for objective skill assessment and for understanding how simple movements are concatenated to form complete procedures, ultimately supporting the development of training and evaluation systems. In this work, we present a pseudo-temporal modeling approach for surgical gesture segmentation using a 2D ResNet-50 convolutional network trained exclusively on RGB frames. During training, each video was divided into eight equal segments, which were used to feed the network and generate gesture-level probabilities. To optimize learning, an adaptive Exponential Moving Average (EMA) mechanism was applied to stabilize weight updates and reinforce poorly performing gesture classes. At inference, complete surgical videos were processed with an overlapping sliding window of eight frames, where each frame could be evaluated multiple times in different contexts. Frame-level probabilities were averaged across overlapping windows and refined through a temporal mode filter, producing stable and coherent gesture sequences. The method was validated on the JIGSAWS dataset, which includes the Suturing, Knot Tying, and Needle Passing tasks. Results showed reliable recognition across all procedures: Suturing achieved 82.26%, Knot Tying 91.97%, and Needle Passing 79.35%. Training required approximately one hour, and inference on a complete video of 2950 frames was performed in about 45 seconds, also generating a labeled output video. By relying solely on 2D convolutions and RGB input, the approach achieves accurate and efficient segmentation with low computational cost, making it suitable for surgical training environments with limited resources and applicable in near real-time scenarios.
Author Keywords: surgical gesture segmentation;pseudo-temporal modeling;2d convolutional neural networks;resnet 50;exponential moving average
IEEE Terms: Training;Adaptation models;Accuracy;Computational modeling;Surgery;Needles;Real-time systems;Windows;Convolutional neural networks;Videos

Document Title: Design of Polymer Optical Waveguides for the Detection of Azo Dye Water Contaminants
Authors: I. M. Garnica-Palafox; E. García-Muñoz; A. M. Velázquez-Benítez; F. M. Sánchez-Arévalo; N. Qureshi
Author Affiliations: Instituto de Ciencias Aplicadas y Tecnología & Instituto de Investigaciones en Materiales Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, CDMX, Mexico; Unidad Zacatenco, Escuela Superior de Ingeniería Mecánica y Eléctrica Instituto Politécnico Nacional, CDMX, México; Instituto de Ciencias Aplicadas y Tecnología Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, CDMX, Mexico; Instituto de Investigaciones en Materiales Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, CDMX, Mexico; Instituto de Ciencias Aplicadas y Tecnología Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, CDMX, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE 42nd Central America and Panama Convention (CONCAPAN XLII)
Abstract: The design of single-mode optical waveguides made of SU-8 photoresist and functionalized with coatings of chitosan-based hydrogels to detect water-polluting azo dyes is presented through analytical and numerical analyses. Two possible waveguide configurations were evaluated in order to obtain the dimensions that allow the maximum sensitivity and facilitate their fabrication through direct laser writing (DLW) lithography method. The sensitivity of the waveguides was studied for TE and TM polarizations at NIR frequencies (λ=975 nm). We systematically explore how both, the hydrated and dehydrated states of the hydrogel receptor/cladding layer, and the waveguide’s dimensions, core width (2.0 < w < 3.0 µm) and height (h = 0.75 and 1.0 µm), influence the sensitivity. The results reveal that TM polarization yields higher sensitivity compared to TE one, particularly in thicker waveguides (h = 1.0 µm). Dehydration of the hydrogel cladding significantly enhances the interaction between the evanescent wave and the hydrogel, leading to a threefold increase in sensitivity which facilitates the detection of pollutant dyes such as acid blue 113 (AB113) or methyl orange (MO). Thus, dehydrated waveguides under TM polarization are highly effective for environmental and biomedical sensing applications, offering a promising approach for the detection of specific contaminants in aqueous solutions.
Author Keywords: Waveguides;SU-8;Chitosan;Poly(vinyl alcohol);Genipin;Hydrogels;Pollutant azo dyes
IEEE Terms: Sensitivity;Optical polarization;Claddings;Biomedical optical imaging;Optical design;Hydrogels;Optical device fabrication;Optical sensors;Optical waveguides;Optical surface waves

Document Title: Robustification of Proportional-Resonant Control Against Frequency Variations
Authors: J. G. Rueda-Escobedo; J. A. Moreno; G. O. Anaya
Author Affiliations: División de Ciencias Básicas, Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), CDMX, Mexico; Eléctrica y Computación, Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), CDMX, Mexico; Eléctrica y Computación, Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), CDMX, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 17th International Workshop on Variable Structure Systems (VSS)
Abstract: Replacing traditional power generation with distributed sources leads to a grid frequency that is more vulnerable to fluctuations. This can impact the efficiency and function of power converters, which are essential for integrating renewable energies into the electrical network. Instead of estimating and tracking the grid’s frequency to counter these changes, this paper proposes a robust proportional-resonant (PR) controller that maintains reference tracking in the presence of a varying frequency. Unlike other approaches, the proposed controller does not require precise knowledge of the frequency. This is made possible by integrating into the PR controller a second-order bi-homogeneous differentiator, significantly improving the controller’s tracking performance. The performance of the proposed approach is demonstrated through numerical simulations involving the operation of a grid-connected inverter during a frequency variation event.
IEEE Terms: Variable structure systems;Time-frequency analysis;Renewable energy sources;Filters;Harmonic analysis;Numerical simulation;Frequency estimation;Frequency control;Power harmonic filters;Power generation

Document Title: Systematic and Robust Tuning of Proportional-Resonant Controllers for Current and Voltage Tracking
Authors: M. A. Estrada; J. G. Rueda-Escobedo; J. A. Moreno; J. Schiffer
Author Affiliations: Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (FIUNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (FIUNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (II-UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Chair of Control Systems and Network Control Technology, Brandenburg University of Technology Cottbus-Senftenberg (BTU C-S), Cottbus, Germany
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE Conference on Control Technology and Applications (CCTA)
Abstract: In the control and operation of power inverters, proportional-resonant (PR) controllers are used to track references and reject disturbances that can be described by a sum of sinusoidal signals of known frequency. The relevance of these controllers is increasing due to the increase in distortion and volatility of three-phase signals in the grid. However, as reported in the literature, the tuning of PR controllers is far from trivial due to their large number of parameters and the presence of pure imaginary poles in the associated transfer function. To address these issues, a time-domain framework based on linear matrix inequalities (LMIs) is presented for the tuning of PR controllers with applications to voltage and current tracking. The advantage of this approach is the straightforward combination with other techniques such as $\mathcal{H}_{\infty}$ control. The effectiveness of the approach is illustrated through numerical simulations, where the injection of a constant active power is achieved in the presence of distorted voltages.
IEEE Terms: Systematics;Transfer functions;Numerical simulation;Distortion;Inverters;Linear matrix inequalities;Delays;Voltage control;Time-domain analysis;Tuning

Document Title: Deep Learning Approach for Aortic Valve Localization, Detection and Segmentation in Computed Tomography Volumes
Authors: R. O. Azuara-Domínguez; J. Olveres; B. Escalante-Ramírez; E. Vallejo
Author Affiliations: Posgrado de Ingeniería Eléctrica, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Centro Médico ABC, Mexico city, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE 38th International Symposium on Computer-Based Medical Systems (CBMS)
Abstract: Standard view cardiac computed tomography (CT) images are of great importance in clinical practice due to the valuable diagnostic information they provide. However, navigating these (3D) medical images to identify such a view can represent a time-consuming process, subject to inter-operator variability. This study presents an approach using a Multiscale Vision Transformer (MViT) to infer the rotation parameters required to obtain standard views and subsequently focus on extracting the left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) view. Once this view is obtained, the aortic valve is detected and segmented using well-known convolutional neural networks (CNNs), specifically ResNet18 for detection and U-Net for segmentation. The methodology used for its training is detailed, along with the metrics employed to evaluate their performance. Finally, the results obtained for each stage are presented. This approach provides a clear understanding of the performance of each model, from rotation parameter prediction to aortic valve detection and segmentation.
Author Keywords: 3D volume Cardiac CT;Deep Learning;Regression;Segmentation;Rotation matrix;Attention
IEEE Terms: Deep learning;Image segmentation;Three-dimensional displays;Computed tomography;Computational modeling;Pipelines;Predictive models;Valves;Data models;Standards

Document Title: A Reasoning System for Graded Modal Logic
Authors: R. López-Villafán; E. Bárcenas
Author Affiliations: IIMAS: Posgrado en Ciencia e lngeniería de la Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingenería: Dpto. de Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 12th International Conference in Software Engineering Research and Innovation (CONISOFT)
Abstract: In this paper, we propose a deduction system for the modal logic $\mathbf{K}$ with graded modalities, which is obtained by adding numerical constraints to the modal operators. Graded modalities can be seen as a generalization of traditional modal operators since they include numerical bounds in the modalities, thus enabling the formal treatment of more detailed expressions, such as “there are at least $n+1$ individuals satisfying a certain property”, where $n$ is a natural number. This kind of properties commonly arise in contexts such as concurrent systems: “if at least 3 processors are free, then proceed to compute the next task”, or epistemic logic:”if more than 2 people know $P$, then $P$ is known”. The system is based on the notion of tree-hypersequents. We show the system is correct, i.e. sound and complete.
Author Keywords: graded modalities;proof theory;hypersequents;automated reasoning
IEEE Terms: Technological innovation;Interpolation;Program processors;Computational modeling;Syntactics;Cognition;Numerical models;Logic;Iterative methods;Formal verification

Document Title: Large-Scale Validation and Analysis of the Rejection Capacity in Entropic Associative Memory
Authors: Á. F. Bórquez; R. Morales; L. A. Pineda
Author Affiliations: Universidad de Sonora, Guadalajara, Jalisco, México; Universidad de Guadalajara, Guadalajara, Jalisco, México; Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Guadalajara, Jalisco, México
Publication Title: 2025 Mexican International Conference on Computer Science (ENC)
Abstract: The Entropic Associative Memory (EAM) is a cognitively inspired computational model that combines probabilistic recognition with an efficient pattern storage and retrieval architecture. Previous evaluations, limited to small domains of about ten classes and a few thousand instances per class, showed promising results in classification and reconstruction. This work extends those studies in two ways. First, we assess scalability using Googles Quick, Draw! dataset, which includes over 300 classes and 100,000 instances per class. Second, we test EAMs rejection capability by storing only half of the classes and evaluating performance on the full dataset. Results confirm EAMs adaptability to large-scale scenarios and its effective rejection of novel stimuli, underscoring its potential as a robust and explainable AI model.

Publication Title: 2025 6th International Conference on Control and Fault-Tolerant Systems (SysTol)
Abstract: We present the implementation of a Cloud-Computing-based system for leak diagnosis in a water distribution network. Pressure head/flow rate measured in field is stored and processed in a virtual machine to provide a leak diagnosis in two stages: 1) first the leak is detected by comparing the current operating conditions and expected nominal operating conditions obtained from a previously adjusted simulation model, and 2) the leak exact location and its magnitude are determined using a meta-heuristic method. The performance of the proposed system is implemented for an experimental hydraulic system at a laboratory scale. Results demonstrate a good accuracy in the leak diagnosis metrics at a reduced economic and computational cost, demonstrating the potential results from the implementation of a similar system in a large-scale water distribution network.

Authors: A. Schaum; S. Koch; J. A. Moreno
Author Affiliations: Chair of Process Analytics, and the Computational Science Hub, University of Hohenheim, Stuttgart, Germany; Institute of Automation and Control, Graz University of Technology, Graz, Austria; Coordinación de Eléetrica y Computación, Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, México
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE 63rd Conference on Decision and Control (CDC)
Abstract: This paper addresses the design of nonlinear quasi-unknown input observers for systems that can be interpreted as an interconnection of a linear dynamical subsystem with a static nonlinear feedback subject to additive perturbations. As assumed classically an exosystem is considered describing the dynamics of the perturbation, meaning that the dynamical mechanisms giving rise to the perturbation are sufficiently well known but the underlying initial condition is unknown. Extending classical results based on the Sylvester equation, and combining them with well-established dissipativity concepts and design methods, a new approach for quasi-unknown input observer design is obtained. It simplifies previous work on general unknown input observer design by exploiting the structural knowledge about the exosystem, without extending the state dimension. The approach is illustrated with numerical case examples.
IEEE Terms: Additives;Perturbation methods;Design methodology;Observers;Mathematical models;Linear matrix inequalities;Nonlinear dynamical systems;Observability;Convergence

Document Title: Fast Coupled Thermoelectric and Magnetic Model to Simulate the Transient Behavior of Inductive and Resistive Superconducting Fault-Current Limiters
Authors: G. d. Santos; F. Trillaud; B. M. O. Santos; F. Sass; G. G. Sotelo
Author Affiliations: Department of Electrical Engineering, State University of Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil; Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Department of Electrical Engineering, State University of Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil; Department of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Federal Fluminense University of Rio de Janeiro, Niterói, Brazil; Department of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Federal Fluminense University of Rio de Janeiro, Niterói, Brazil
Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Applied Superconductivity
Abstract: The nonlinear dependence of the resistance of high-temperature superconductors (HTSs) on current, temperature, and magnetic flux density is the basis of the operation of some superconducting power equipment. By modeling the resistance, to simulate in detail the transient behavior of such devices in power systems. In that regard, the finest and most accurate models employ the finite-element method (FEM). Nevertheless, the most common approach in power systems remains the lumped parameters using a classic thermal–electrical analogy. Thus far, there has not been a general method that can combine fine geometrical features of the superconducting device given by finite-element models and the flexibility and computational speed of lumped-parameter models (LPMs). A new model is proposed here to bridge both modeling philosophies and benefit from their advantages. This model, referred to as finite-element lumped-parameter model (FELPM), couples an FEM with an LPM in the same framework. The resulting FELPM is compared with two existing LPMs for the two main topologies of superconducting fault-current limiters (SFCL): resistive (RSFCL) and inductive (ISFCL). All the models are validated against experimental data from the literature when available. It is shown that the new approach yields reasonable computation times, especially in the case of inductive superconducting fault-current limiters. It is argued that such a proposal could be expanded to analyze transients of superconducting power components in more complex electrical networks.

Document Title: Novel VSC-Based STATCOM Model Based on Dynamic Phasors for Unbalanced Distribution Networks
Authors: J. H. Sanchez; L. M. Castro
Author Affiliations: Posgrado en Ingeniería Eléctrica, National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Electrical Energy, National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
Abstract: This paper presents the dynamic phasor-based modeling of three-phase voltage-source converters (VSC) operating as static var compensators (STATCOM). The generalized averaging theory is used to represent the DC, 1st, and 2nd harmonics of the converter fundamental response. The VSC model is characterized by the use of basic power system elements. On the AC side, it consists of three wye-connected, single-phase complex transformers whose taps replicate the AC-to-DC energy conversion of the converter commanded by the modulating variables. On the DC side, a capacitor is connected in parallel with a current source to reproduce the  $2{\omega }$  effects of the network unbalances. Dynamic controllers based on symmetrical components were developed for the VSC to operate as STATCOM. All these aspects distinguish the proposed VSC model from the switching-based model (SBM) and averaged-value model (AVM). The model’s dynamic behavior was validated by comparing it to the SBM and AVM, using a five-bus, three-phase network taken from Simulink/Simscape Electrical. Root mean square errors were smaller than 5.56% and 5.53%, with the new model being 43 and 17 times more computationally efficient than the SBM and AVM, respectively. The IEEE 34-bus test system with two STATCOM was also studied to verify the model practicality. Short-circuit faults were assessed to prove that both STATCOM suitably perform under unbalanced operating conditions.
Author Keywords: Dynamic phasors;unbalanced three-phase power networks;VSC modeling;VSC-based STATCOM
IEEE Terms: Automatic voltage control;Harmonic analysis;Integrated circuit modeling;Voltage control;Power system dynamics;Computational modeling;Power system harmonics

Document Title: A Framework for Dynamic User Modeling Integrating Data Stream Mining and Process Mining in Educational Contexts
Authors: M. Yesenia Zavaleta-Sanchez; E. Benitez-Guerrero; G. Gilberto Molero-Castillo; C. Mezura-Godoy; L. Gerardo Montane-Jimenez
Author Affiliations: Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad Veracruzana (UV), Xalapa, Heroica Veracruz, Mexico; Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad Veracruzana (UV), Xalapa, Heroica Veracruz, Mexico; Departamento de Computación, Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad Veracruzana (UV), Xalapa, Heroica Veracruz, Mexico; Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad Veracruzana (UV), Xalapa, Heroica Veracruz, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Access
Abstract: A user model (UM) is a representation of the characteristics, behaviors, and preferences of a user that a computer system utilizes to provide the user with personalized experiences. In educational settings, for instance, a system can use information about the Learning Styles (LSs) of a student to provide them with adequate study materials. Furthermore, with the growing use of Learning Management System (LMS) platforms, such as Moodle, continuous streams of user data highlight the need to compute student models dynamically to support adaptive decision-making in teaching and learning. This paper introduces a framework for Dynamic User Modeling (DUMING) to create evolving Dynamic User Models (DUMs) that capture and adapt to changes in student LSs over time, based on event log streams from an LMS. This framework leverages the Felder and Silverman Learning Styles Model (FSLSM) and integrates Data Stream Mining (DSM) and Process Mining (PM) techniques across three layers: collection, preprocessing, and mining. Collected event streams are processed within landmark windows and classified into High-Level Events (HLEs) based on LMS functionality. The STREAMKMeans algorithm clusters events, and the PM phase uses Alpha Miner (AM), Heuristic Miner (HM), and Inductive Miner (IM) algorithms to identify learning paths represented as Petri Nets (PNs), with IM showing the highest values in the quality metrics used. Implemented and evaluated in a course using Moodle at the Universidad Veracruzana in Mexico, the framework employs IF-THEN rules to relate HLE proportions to FSLSM-based LSs, visualizing changes through radar charts. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework in generating adaptive DUMs, providing insights that enhance over time educational support and enable timely interventions.

Document Title: Detection of DoS and DDoS Attacks Using LSTM in Computer Networks: A Systematic Literature Review
Authors: A. E. Godínez; J. L. Garza Rivera; D. T. Varela
Author Affiliations: CDMX, Universidad Da Vinci, México; CDMX, Universidad Da Vinci, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores Cuautitlán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Cuautitlan Izcalli, México
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE Latin American School on Computational Intelligence and Robotics (SAIBOT)
Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI), with machine learning (ML) models and deep learning (DL) neural networks, represents a possibility for detecting DoS/DDoS attacks more effectively. The LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) model is one of the options for detecting these increasingly varied and sophisticated attacks. The objective of this research was to evaluate the application of the LSTM model in the detection of DoS and DDoS cyber attacks on communications networks. This research was designed as a Systematic Literature Review to obtain the most recent studies on the subject. As a result, 86 initial studies were obtained from the databases. Once the entire procedure, including the quality assessment of the studies, was completed, 15 final primary studies were selected. With these primary studies, it was possible to answer all the questions in the review. It should be noted that modern SDN (Software Defined Network) networks, Cloud Computing networks, and classic networks are threatened by this type of attack. The LSTM models applied in the detection of DoS/DDoS attacks can be hybrid or combined. The most commonly used evaluation metrics are Accuracy, Recall, Precision, and F1-Score, and the most frequently found public datasets include NSL-KDD, CICDoS2019, and CICIDS2017. A wide variety of LSTM-based models and approaches that can be used to experiment and compare their performance in terms of communications network security.
Author Keywords: Long short term memory;Systematic literature review;Machine learning;Denial-of-service attack;Network security
IEEE Terms: Measurement;Computational modeling;Denial-of-service attack;NSL-KDD;Market research;Communication networks;Software defined networking;Long short term memory;Cyberattack;Systematic literature review

Authors: A. I. H. Al-Jarah; J. L. O. Arjona

Publication Title: 2024 IEEE 15th Annual Ubiquitous Computing, Electronics & Mobile Communication Conference (UEMCON)
Abstract: Computation has mostly used parallelism for high-performance computation, and multicore processors have gained popularity because they offer better processing time. However, one of the most challenging tasks to achieve is securely exchange confidential data over the network between sender and receiver. The research work in this paper explains how secret key steganography, combined with parallel processing, is utilized to conceal secret data within an image. The proposed algorithm describes and focuses on the generation method of a secret key while merging it with the Least Significant Bit (LSB) algorithm to achieve robustness, as well as parallel computing to improve the processing time in both the encoding/decoding phases. The proposed algorithm adds another layer of security by adding a robustness parameter (secret key), convenient for keeping secret, confidential, and crucial data. Parallel processing, combined with the new method to formulate the secret key is the novelty and strongest attribute of the proposed algorithm. When parallel computing is applied, the process speeds up compared with the same algorithm in the sequential version. The results are compared with the LSB algorithm in terms of the peak signal to noise ratio (PSNR) and mean square error (MSE) values, aspiring to present that the proposed algorithm adds robustness without affecting image imperceptibility.
Author Keywords: Hiding data;Image steganography;Processing time improvement;Parallel steganography;Secret key steganography;Steganography

Abstract: This paper presents a machine learning-based approach for analyzing and predicting the behavior of electrical frequency in the El Salvador power grid using data collected from a Frequency Disturbance Recorder (FDR). The research is based on previous studies on the statistical and temporal analysis of frequency data, integrating predictive modeling techniques to improve understanding and forecasting capabilities. A Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network was implemented to process high-resolution time-series data collected between September 2022 and June 2023. Data were preprocessed by resampling, normalization, and conversion to optimize computational efficiency. The LSTM model demonstrated strong predictive capabilities, particularly at smaller time intervals, achieving a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.01 and a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.8375 with resampled data of 6 seconds. However, limitations in the performance of the model were observed with resampled data on an hourly basis, highlighting challenges in capturing long-term dependencies. The study contributes to ongoing efforts to utilize advanced machine learning techniques to monitor the power of the system and emphasizes the potential for scalable and efficient predictive solutions.

Author Affiliations: UDEE-F1, UNaM, Oberá, Argentina; UDEE-F1, UNaM-CONICET, Oberá, Argentina; GEA-IITEMA, UNRC-CONICET, Río Cuarto, Argentina; FI-UBA, Instituto de Ingenierta Biomedica, Buenos Aires, Argentina
Publication Title: 2025 XXI Workshop on Information Procesing and Control (RPIC)
Abstract: This paper presents a strategy for detecting interturn short-circuit (ITSC) faults in permanent magnet synchronous machines (PMSM) based on tracking the fundamental component of a residual voltage using the Vold-Kalman filter (VKF). The vector models of the voltages at the midpoint of the phase windings for a faulty PMSM are presented. In addition, the orthogonal vector (OV) model of the VKF algorithm used to compute the magnitude and phase shift from the measured voltage is described. Experimental results for a PMSM with ITSC of varying severity under both no-load and full-load conditions are presented. It is demonstrated that a ITSC fault can be reliably detected from 1 % fault severity, and the corresponding faulty winding can be accurately identified.

Authors: J. M. Hernández Duarte; A. Y. Alarcón López; E. R. Ángeles Anguiano
Author Affiliations: Universidad Da Vinci, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Departamento de Ciencias Quimicas, FES Cuautitlán - UNAM, Cuautitlán Izcalli, Mexico; Departamento de Ciencias Quimicas, FES Cuautitlán - UNAM, Cuautitlán Izcalli, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE Latin American School on Computational Intelligence and Robotics (SAIBOT)
Abstract: Drug discovery is an intricate and costly process, in which the accurate prediction of protein-ligand binding affinity (BAP) represents a critical step. This systematic review article addresses the state of the art in this field, focusing on the evolution and optimization of deep learning (DL) architectures that have emerged as a robust alternative to traditional computational and experimental methods. A taxonomy is proposed that classifies DL models based on their data representation: structure-based models (such as convolutional and graph neural networks), sequence-based models, and hybrid and multimodal approaches. Comparative analysis highlights the superiority of architectures that integrate multimodal information and employ attention mechanisms, demonstrating improved predictive accuracy and generalization capabilities. Nonetheless, there are still issues, such as the limited interpretability of the model or the bias and scarcity of training data. Finally, future research directions emphasize the revolutionary potential of quantum computing, which promises to overcome computational and data limitations, and generative artificial intelligence, which enables de novo molecular design. This work directs the scientific community to the most promising research areas by offering a conceptual framework for comprehending the state of DL-driven BAP today.

Authors: M. A. Zaragoza-Luquín; K. Gutierrez-Calvillo; R. Dominguez-Navarrete; F. J. Ruiz-Hernandez; C. Pérez-Rojas; M. R. A. Paternina; A. Zamora
Author Affiliations: Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; UNAM, Mexico City; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE PES Generation, Transmission and Distribution Latin America Conference and Industrial Exposition (GTDLA)
Abstract: This paper proposes an alternative way of designing multiple power system stabilizers (PSSs) for damping electromechanical modes. The approach is based on a system identification method that allows the computation of the first-order sensitivities of the eigenvalues of power system linear models. Thus, the eigensystem realization algorithm (ERA) is used to identify the power system linear model. This proposal derives a state-space representation using power system measurements to compute the power system eigenvalues and their residues for designing a single PSS for damping one mode or coordinated multiple PSSs for damping several modes. The attained results in two benchmark systems, Kundur and the New England transmission system-New York power system (NETS-NYPS), demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed strategy in improving the dynamic response and power system stability for damping one or several electromechanical modes in large power systems, reaching stable behaviors in less than 2s and 7s for the Kundur and NETS-NYPS grids, respectively.

Abstract: This paper introduces the development of detailed and average models for a battery-based energy storage system (ESS) tailored for electromagnetic transient (EMT) studies using ATP-EMTP. The models, designed with TACS and MODEL elements, include a non-isolated bidirectional DC-DC boost converter and a three-phase, two-level voltage source converter, each represented in detailed and average forms. Simulation results highlight the strengths of each model: the detailed model excels in accurately reproducing high-frequency switching phenomena, while the average model provides computational efficiency by focusing on low-frequency dynamics. These findings demonstrate the trade-off between precision and speed, offering insights for applications ranging from detailed analysis to large-scale grid simulations.

Publication Title: 2024 International Conference on Electrical Machines (ICEM)
Abstract: In this paper is analyzed the effect of winding type on the performance of three-phase radial-flux permanent magnet synchronous generators for applications in wind turbines. The winding types considered for designing these generators are the concentric, integer and distributed windings. Moreover, a generator virtual prototype is obtained from each winding in software CAD. Then, each virtual prototype is simulated through 2D and 3D finite element analysis of each winding type to test performance variables such as the copper mass, losses, the power quality and, cogging and ripple torques. Finally, the performance results for these generator designs are shown in a comparative table and also, the main winding features are described for the purpose of being used in wind turbines.

Document Title: Students’ Collaborative Learning through Programming Computational Intelligence Algorithms: A Mixed Methods Approach Applied to a Case Study on Energy and Buildings
Authors: R. Tariq; I. Sánchez-Domínguez; A. J. Argüelles Cruz
Author Affiliations: Tecnologico de Monterrey, Institute for the Future of Education, Monterrey, N.L., Mexico; Unidad Académica del IIMAS en el Estado de Yucatán–UNAM, Mérida, Yucatán, Mexico; Instituto Politecnico Nacional, CIC, Mexico City, CDMX, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE Latin American Conference on Computational Intelligence (LA-CCI)
Abstract: In response to the global demand for sustainable energy systems and the need to equip future professionals with computational intelligence (CI) skills, this study investigates how collaborative programming of CI algorithms can support student learning in the context of building energy consumption prediction. This research contributes to the limited literature on collaborative CI learning by integrating domain-specific energy data and structured team-based programming, demonstrating a viable instructional model for enhancing algorithmic reasoning in sustainability education. A convergent mixed-methods case study was conducted as an exploratory research study with 14 undergraduate students enrolled in a Data Science course at Tecnológico de Monterrey. Students worked in teams to develop and optimize machine learning and CI models (e.g., ANN, LSTM, GPR) for predicting annual energy use in school buildings. Quantitative metrics such as RMSE and R2 (reported to be R2=0.979 for training and R2=0.981 for testing, coupled with low RMSE=20,504 for training and RMSE=19,948 for testing which were the results of one of the students) assessed model performance, while a structured survey captured cognitive and collaborative learning outcomes. Results revealed that the activity improved students’ understanding of hyperparameter tuning and model evaluation. High levels of engagement were observed, particularly in statistical analysis and data visualization tasks. However, collaborative dynamics yielded mixed perceptions; while many students appreciated teamwork, others encountered challenges in coordination and equitable task division. Qualitative responses emphasized computational constraints and suggested greater support for model integration and real-world relevance. It is concluded that integrating collaborative programming of computational intelligence algorithms into real-world energy prediction tasks effectively supports the development of students’ data science competencies, while highlighting the need for structured team coordination to ensure balanced participation and learning outcomes.
Author Keywords: collaborative programming;computational intelligence;machine learning education;energy consumption;educational innovation;higher education;mixed methods
IEEE Terms: Training;Federated learning;Computational modeling;Buildings;Collaboration;Prediction algorithms;Data models;Programming profession;Computational intelligence;Tuning
